title: Eschatology: Death to the Second Coming — WCF 32.1
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: Lesson 2 on Eschatology_ Death to the Second Coming - WCF 32.1 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Context: The Bible Presbyterian Church and the Westminster Standards
subpoints: The BPC was founded in 1938, the first American body to formally amend the Westminster Standards
Amendments conformed the Confession (ch. 32-33) and Larger Catechism (Q. 82, 84-90) to a premillennial return of Christ
A resolution was also passed affirming freedom for officers to hold other views provided they affirm the physical return of Christ
This denominational context shapes how the amended WLC Q. 86 will be taught
A seminary historian noted the BPC is 'the only honest Presbyterian body' for amending rather than ignoring disagreed-with standards

outline.title: Westminster Confession of Faith 32.1 — The State After Death
subpoints: The bodies of men after death return to dust and see corruption
Souls are immortal and immediately return to God who gave them
Souls of the righteous are made perfect in holiness and received into the highest heavens, beholding the face of God
Souls of the wicked are cast into hell, remaining in torments and utter darkness
Scripture acknowledges no other place for souls — no purgatory, no third option
No reincarnation: appointed to men once to die, then judgment (Hebrews 9:27)

outline.title: Biblical Vocabulary for the Intermediate State
subpoints: Sheol (Hebrew): place of the departed, used for both righteous and wicked — simply the place of the dead
Hades (Greek): equivalent of Sheol; the New Testament parallel; not the multi-level underworld of Greek mythology
Paradise: Persian word for garden; only the righteous go there; equated with the third heaven in 2 Corinthians 12
Abraham's bosom: term from Luke 16 for the blessed state of Lazarus
Heaven: rarely used for the intermediate state in the Bible; usually refers to where God and angels dwell
Gehenna / lake of fire: New Testament term for hell — derived from the Valley of Hinnom, the always-burning garbage dump outside Jerusalem

outline.title: The Happiness of the Righteous in the Intermediate State
subpoints: Souls in heaven are freed from sin and misery, in communion with Christ, waiting for the resurrection
The American church has become quasi-Gnostic — treating the intermediate state as the ultimate goal rather than the resurrection
The intermediate state is good but temporary; we are body-and-soul creatures designed for physical existence
Luke 16: Lazarus is comforted; Luke 23: the thief goes to paradise on that day
Philippians 1:21-23: Paul desires to depart and be with Christ, which is far better, yet chooses to remain for the church
Hebrews 12: the general assembly of spirits of just men made perfect
Revelation 14:13: the dead who die in the Lord rest from their labors

outline.title: The Nature of Existence in the Intermediate State
subpoints: The righteous dead are described as souls or spirits — not yet embodied — yet personal pronouns indicate continuing personality
The intermediate state is consciously experienced by both the righteous and the wicked; no soul sleep
Revelation 6:9-11: martyrs under the throne cry out, asking how long until the resurrection — they long to be reunited with their bodies
2 Corinthians 5:1-5: Paul does not want to be 'naked' (without a body); the intermediate state is not the end goal
The wicked in hell are also in a conscious holding period — judged but awaiting final sentencing at the resurrection
Revelation 20:5: the rest of the dead do not live again until the thousand years are finished
John 5: two resurrections — resurrection of life and resurrection of condemnation

summary:
Pastor Tito Lyro opens this second lesson by providing important historical context: the Bible Presbyterian Church was founded in 1938 and was the first American Presbyterian body to formally amend the Westminster Standards, revising Confession chapters 32-33 and Larger Catechism questions 82 and 84-90 to align with a premillennial return of Christ. Alongside those amendments, the founding General Synod passed a resolution preserving freedom of conscience for officers on eschatological matters, provided they affirm the visible, physical return of Christ and do not contradict the system of doctrine in the standards.

The doctrinal heart of the lesson is Westminster Confession of Faith 32.1, which teaches that after death the body returns to dust while the soul immediately returns to God. The righteous are made perfect in holiness and enter the highest heavens to behold the face of God; the wicked are cast into hell in torment. There is no purgatory and no reincarnation — only two destinations, confirmed by Hebrews 9:27 ('appointed once to die, then judgment'). Pastor Lyro surveys the biblical vocabulary for this period: Sheol, Hades, Paradise, Abraham's bosom, heaven, and Gehenna.

For the righteous, the intermediate state means freedom from sin and misery, close communion with Christ, and conscious waiting for the resurrection. However, the pastor cautions against over-idealizing this period — the American church has become quasi-Gnostic in treating the soul's state in heaven as the ultimate destination. Scripture consistently treats the bodily resurrection as the ultimate hope: Revelation 6 shows the martyrs actively longing for it, and 2 Corinthians 5 shows Paul regarding the bodiless state as 'nakedness' he does not desire permanently.

For the wicked, the intermediate state is a conscious holding period — like a convicted criminal awaiting sentencing. Using Revelation 20:4-5 and John 5:28-29, Pastor Lyro defends two future resurrections: the resurrection of the righteous at Christ's return and the resurrection of the wicked at the close of the millennial reign, after which comes the final judgment.
